stage.name: P-Mesh and integration boundaries: requirements, scope, and caveats
stage.description: A boundary lesson for multi-core interface language and responsible system-level CVA6 claims.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

scenes.title: System integration is where over-claiming gets expensive

content.title: System integration is where over-claiming gets expensive
content.subtitle: TRI, P-Mesh, PMA, and cache boundaries must be taught as source-scoped claims, not marketing fog.
content.thesis: A good integration lesson separates requirements, implemented applicability, and system responsibility.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements: interface requirements
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements document names MEM, DBG, IRQ, XIF, and TRI requirements, including AXI memory interface, CLINT interrupt interface, CV-X-IF, and P-Mesh TRI.

content.speakerNotes: This lesson is P-Mesh and integration boundaries: requirements, scope, and caveats. TRI, P-Mesh, PMA, and cache boundaries must be taught as source-scoped claims, not marketing fog. The point is not to memorize a wall of CVA6 facts. The point is to build a disciplined source-reading habit: name the boundary, name the source, and avoid claims that the public material does not support.

actions.text: This lesson is P-Mesh and integration boundaries: requirements, scope, and caveats. TRI, P-Mesh, PMA, and cache boundaries must be taught as source-scoped claims, not marketing fog. The point is not to memorize a wall of CVA6 facts. The point is to build a disciplined source-reading habit: name the boundary, name the source, and avoid claims that the public material does not support.

scenes.title: Requirement language and applicability can diverge

content.title: Read the requirement, then read the applicability table
content.subtitle: The requirements mention TRI for P-Mesh; the interface page marks current configurations carefully.

columns.title: Requirement intent
columns.body: TRI is named as the interface needed for P-Mesh coherence system integration.

columns.title: Applicability check
columns.body: The interface page states TRI is exclusive of AXI and marks listed configurations as no TRI interface.

content.takeaway: Read requirements and implementation applicability together.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements: interface requirements
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements document names MEM, DBG, IRQ, XIF, and TRI requirements, including AXI memory interface, CLINT interrupt interface, CV-X-IF, and P-Mesh TRI.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: Interfaces
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The user manual lists AXI, Debug, Interrupt, and TRI interface applicability across CVA6 configurations.

content.speakerNotes: This lesson is a good example of mature source reading. The requirements document names TRI for P-Mesh coherence integration. The interface page then says TRI is exclusive of AXI and marks listed configurations as no TRI interface. Those statements are not useless or contradictory if read correctly. They tell us to distinguish product requirement intent from current configuration applicability.

actions.text: This lesson is a good example of mature source reading. The requirements document names TRI for P-Mesh coherence integration. The interface page then says TRI is exclusive of AXI and marks listed configurations as no TRI interface. Those statements are not useless or contradictory if read correctly. They tell us to distinguish product requirement intent from current configuration applicability.

scenes.title: PMA is the local region-semantics guardrail

content.title: Before saying coherence, first ask region semantics

items.title: Non-idempotent
items.body: Some regions can have side effects on access and must be treated carefully.

items.title: Executable
items.body: Instruction fetch permission is a region property, not a guess.

items.title: Cacheable
items.body: Cacheability is a declared property that shapes memory behavior.

items.title: Coherence
items.body: Standalone CVA6 does not let us invent a coherence protocol.

content.takeaway: PMA keeps memory claims local and precise.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: PMA
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: PMA describes non-idempotent, executable, and cacheable regions and reminds readers not to invent coherence behavior for standalone CVA6.

content.speakerNotes: PMA is the guardrail before big system claims. A region may be non-idempotent, executable, or cacheable, and those are real properties. But that does not let us invent full coherence behavior for standalone CVA6. If a learner remembers one thing: cacheability and coherence are not synonyms.

actions.text: PMA is the guardrail before big system claims. A region may be non-idempotent, executable, or cacheable, and those are real properties. But that does not let us invent full coherence behavior for standalone CVA6. If a learner remembers one thing: cacheability and coherence are not synonyms.

scenes.title: The integration chapter is a TODO, and that matters

content.title: Incomplete documentation is also source evidence
content.subtitle: The core-integration chapter openly asks for contributors and lists intended topics.

cards.title: Instantiation
cards.body: How to instantiate CVA6 into a core complex or SoC.

cards.title: Reset and clocking
cards.body: Reset styles, clock gating, and timing assumptions need integration care.

cards.title: ASIC/FPGA guidance
cards.body: Implementation targets introduce different RAM, timing, and critical-path concerns.

cards.title: Verification scope
cards.body: Requirements and verification plans define what the IP is expected to cover.

content.takeaway: When docs are incomplete, say so and narrow the claim.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: Core Integration
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The integration chapter is explicitly incomplete and lists intended integration topics such as instantiation, resets, clocking, timing, ASIC, and FPGA guidance.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements: scope and verification intent
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements document defines the CVA6 feature scope and ties requirements to verification intent and interface requirements.

content.speakerNotes: Incomplete documentation is not something to hide. The core-integration chapter explicitly says the team is looking for contributors and lists topics such as instantiation, reset, clocking, ASIC, and FPGA guidance. For our course, this is a feature: it teaches learners to say what is known, what is planned, and what needs direct project review.

actions.text: Incomplete documentation is not something to hide. The core-integration chapter explicitly says the team is looking for contributors and lists topics such as instantiation, reset, clocking, ASIC, and FPGA guidance. For our course, this is a feature: it teaches learners to say what is known, what is planned, and what needs direct project review.

scenes.title: Check understanding: P-Mesh and integration boundaries: requirements, scope, and caveats

questions.question: What is the safest reading of TRI/P-Mesh material?

options.label: Separate requirement intent from current configuration applicability
Assume every CVA6 config has TRI
Assume AXI and TRI are identical

questions.analysis: The requirements and interface applicability tables must be read together.

questions.question: Which PMA categories are useful before making memory-system claims?
questions.analysis: RAS depth is a frontend predictor detail, not a PMA category.

questions.question: What should an incomplete integration chapter cause you to do?

options.label: Narrow the public claim and mark follow-up review
Assume no SoC can use CVA6

questions.analysis: Incomplete documentation is a reason for careful scope, not imagination.

questions.question: Which phrase is most professional?

options.label: This behavior is configuration-scoped and should be checked against integration sources
All CVA6 systems are fully coherent by default
TRI always replaces every memory topic

questions.analysis: System-level claims require configuration and integration evidence.

content.speakerNotes: This quiz checks the core judgment of P-Mesh and integration boundaries: requirements, scope, and caveats. The right answers should feel less like trivia and more like disciplined classification: what is the boundary, what is the source, what is the limitation, and what would we check next?

scenes.title: Summary: P-Mesh and integration boundaries: requirements, scope, and caveats

content.title: P-Mesh and integration boundaries: requirements, scope, and caveats
takeaways: Separate requirement intent, current interface applicability, PMA attributes, cache boundaries, and integration TODOs.
Turn every public explanation into an evidence-backed learning artifact.
content.nextStep: Next: Unit 08 checkpoint, where learners classify AXI, interrupt/debug, CV-X-IF, PMA, and integration questions.
content.closing: This is how OpenHW Explorer can teach serious open hardware without drifting into unsupported certainty.

content.speakerNotes: To summarize: Separate requirement intent, current interface applicability, PMA attributes, cache boundaries, and integration TODOs. Keep the claim small, keep the source visible, and keep the boundary honest. Next: Unit 08 checkpoint, where learners classify AXI, interrupt/debug, CV-X-IF, PMA, and integration questions.
